runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 3 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Gruber and Saez (2002) - Elasticity of taxable income
CBO microsimulation documentation - labor supply elasticities by income group
Immervoll et al. (2007) - EUROMOD/US tax-benefit microsimulation parameterizations
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners typically have low substitution elasticities (0.1–0.3 range). Decile 3 represents relatively low-earning primary earners, who may have slightly higher elasticities than middle/high earners due to proximity to participation margins and EITC phase-in/phase-out regions. However, as primary earners (not secondary), the intensive-margin substitution response is modest. Standard microsimulation models (e.g., CBO, TRIM, TAXSIM-adjacent tools) typically calibrate these coefficients drawing on Gruber-Saez type estimates. For low-income primary earners, values around 0.10–0.25 are common parameterizations. I center at 0.15 with moderate uncertainty reflecting variation across model specifications and estimation strategies.

(2007) - EUROMOD/US tax-benefit microsimulation parameterizations"], "reasoning_summary": "Primary earners typically have low substitution elasticities (0.1\u20130.3 range). Standard microsimulation models (e.g., CBO, TRIM, TAXSIM-adjacent tools) typically calibrate these coefficients drawing on Gruber-Saez type estimates. For low-income primary earners, values around 0.10\u20130.25 are common parameterizations. I center at 0.15 with moderate uncertainty reflecting variation across model specifications and estimation strategies."}

citations: Gruber and Saez (2002) - Elasticity of Taxable Income

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners generally have low labor supply elasticities. For decile 3 (relatively low earnings), the substitution elasticity is expected to be modestly higher than for median/high earners because low-income workers face EITC phase-in/phase-out regions and may have more margin for adjustment. Typical microsimulation models (e.g., CBO, TRIM, TAXSIM-adjacent) calibrate primary-earner substitution elasticities in the range of 0.1–0.3 for lower deciles. The canonical Gruber-Saez taxable income elasticity of ~0.12–0.40 provides a broad anchor; for primary earners specifically (intensive margin, substitution effect only) in decile 3, values around 0.10–0.25 are most plausible, with a central estimate near 0.15.

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 3 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Gruber and Saez (2002) - Elasticity of Taxable Income
CBO (2012) - How the Supply of Labor Responds to Changes in Fiscal Policy
Immervoll et al. (2007) - EUROMOD microsimulation substitution elasticities
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners generally have low labor supply elasticities. For decile 3 (relatively low earnings), the substitution elasticity is somewhat higher than for middle/upper deciles because lower earners have more margin for adjustment and face EITC phase-in/phase-out regions that amplify effective wage changes. However, as primary earners (not secondary), the elasticity remains modest. Microsimulation models like TAXSIM or CBO's model typically calibrate substitution elasticities for primary earners in the range of 0.1–0.3, with lower deciles toward the higher end of that range. I center my estimate at 0.15, reflecting a moderate substitution response for low-earning primary earners, with uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05 to 0.35.

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 3 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Gruber and Saez (2002) - Elasticity of taxable income estimates
CBO microsimulation documentation - labor supply elasticities by income group
Immervoll et al. (2007) - EUROMOD/tax-benefit simulation labor supply parameters
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners generally have low labor supply elasticities. For decile 3 (relatively low earnings), the substitution elasticity is expected to be modestly higher than for middle/upper deciles because lower-earning primary earners may have somewhat more margin to adjust hours. However, being primary earners constrains the elasticity to remain small. Standard microsimulation models (e.g., CBO, TAXSIM-linked models) typically calibrate primary-earner substitution elasticities in the range of 0.1–0.3, with lower-decile workers sometimes assigned slightly higher values than median workers. Gruber and Saez's taxable income elasticity estimates and CBO's assumed labor supply parameters anchor my belief around 0.15, with meaningful uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05 to 0.35.

citations: Gruber and Saez (2002) - Elasticity of Taxable Income

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners generally have low substitution elasticities (typically 0.1–0.3 in the literature). Decile 3 represents relatively low-earning primary earners. In microsimulation models calibrated to U.S. data, primary-earner substitution elasticities are usually set lower than secondary earners. For lower deciles, elasticities can be modestly higher than the middle due to proximity to participation margins and EITC phase-in/phase-out regions, but the substitution (intensive margin) component alone is typically modest. CBO and similar models often use values in the 0.1–0.25 range for primary earners. I center my estimate at 0.15 with moderate uncertainty reflecting variation across model specifications and the specific decile-level calibration.
